question: Identify the root cause of the network fault: which device, what changed, and why it breaks.
description:
R3 changes `bgp cluster-id 1` to `bgp cluster-id 7`. R7 keeps cluster-id
1. The two RRs now have *different* cluster-IDs, breaking the shared-
cluster loop-prevention pattern: routes reflected by R7 are no longer
recognized by R3 as already-reflected, so R3 re-reflects them, and vice
versa. Each iBGP-learned prefix is now received twice by PE clients
with different cluster-list contents → unnecessary path churn, possible
best-path oscillation, and double the BGP table size.
expected_rca:
Root cause: on 100.64.0.103 (R3), bgp cluster-id was changed from 1 to 7 while R7 (the other RR) keeps cluster-id 1.
With mismatched cluster-IDs the shared-cluster loop prevention breaks: each RR no longer recognizes the other's reflected routes as already-reflected, so they re-reflect each other's routes. PE clients receive each iBGP prefix twice with differing cluster-lists, causing path churn, possible best-path oscillation, and a doubled BGP table.
Fix: set R3's bgp cluster-id back to 1.
expected_symptoms_summary:
BGP session state change (%ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE):
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.

apply.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
bgp cluster-id 7

revert.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
bgp cluster-id 1

expected_symptoms: RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:28:01.053 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.3 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:28:02.675 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.3 Down - Peer closing down the session (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:28:07.658 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.3 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
